lessons.title: Why Rockets Need Fins
lessons.body: Fins keep a rocket flying straight by pushing against the air, just like feathers on a dart. Without enough fin area, a rocket can tumble instead of flying up.

lessons.title: Thrust vs Weight
lessons.body: A rocket only lifts off if its engines push harder (thrust) than gravity pulls down (weight). Engineers call this the thrust-to-weight ratio.

lessons.title: Fuel and Oxidizer
lessons.body: Fire needs oxygen. In space there's no air, so rockets carry their own oxidizer (like liquid oxygen) to burn fuel.

lessons.title: Two-Stage Rockets
lessons.body: Orbital rockets often use two stages. The big first stage lifts off the ground, then falls away so a lighter second stage can finish the trip to orbit.

lessons.title: Recovery Systems
lessons.body: Parachutes slow a rocket down so it lands safely and can be reused, just like Estes model rockets and SpaceX boosters.

lessons.title: Guidance Systems
lessons.body: Guidance computers use sensors (and sometimes GPS) to keep a rocket pointed the right way during flight.
